nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; found under rocky overhangs, in caves with sponges, and beneath large plate corals in sheltered inner reefs and tide pools

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs

diet.notes: Primarily targets live copepods and zooplankton; in the wild has been documented acting as a cleaner fish, removing parasites and dead tissue from other reef species. In captivity, generally advised to provide a steady supply of live copepods from a mature reef or pod culture. Has been reported to accept frozen Mysis, Cyclop-Eeze, Nutramar Ova, and vitamin-enriched baby brine shrimp once acclimated, though live foods tend to be strongly preferred, especially initially. Captive-bred individuals tend to accept prepared foods more readily.

imageCandidates.notes: Dan Schofield; 1024x768; striking sharp in-situ shot of a single vibrant individual showing the diagnostic blue snout/head stripe, vivid orange-red body and dark tail; small corner signature watermark only.

Pierre Pericard (via GBIF); 2048x1638; clean, no-watermark reef photo clearly showing the blue head, orange lateral stripe and the diagnostic black-and-white paddle-shaped tail fin; subject a little small in frame.
Dan Schofield; 1024x768; sharp side profile of a single individual, orange body with blue head and white-tipped tail; different angle for variety; corner signature watermark.

summary: A slender, expert-level saltwater pipefish with a vivid orange body and blue head, generally kept in species-only or seahorse tanks. Primarily feeds on live copepods and demands a mature, low-flow reef with ample live rock.
careNotes:
Janss' Pipefish is generally considered one of the more approachable pipefish for experienced reef keepers, though it remains classified as expert-only by most retailers due to its specialized feeding requirements. It has been known to exhibit cleaner fish behavior in aquariums, visiting other tank inhabitants to remove ectoparasites — a behavior observed in the wild where it maintains established cleaning stations frequented by cardinalfish and damselfish.

This species tends to prefer low water flow and is generally advised to be housed away from powerheads and strong circulation. It spends most of its time swimming in the mid-water column beneath overhangs and among live rock, rarely resting on the substrate. Males carry the eggs in a ventral brood pouch beneath the tail and give birth to live young after development inside the egg — a reproductive mode known as ovoviviparity.

Captive-bred individuals have been reported to live up to 5 years with proper care; wild-caught specimens often fare more poorly, with lifespan estimates of 2–3 years more common in the literature. A species-only or seahorse-dominated setup is strongly recommended to eliminate competition for food from faster, more aggressive feeders.
breedingNotes: Males brood the eggs in a ventral pouch beneath the tail and give birth to live young after development inside the egg — a reproductive mode known as ovoviviparity. Breeding in captivity has been reported but is considered difficult, and captive-bred specimens are only occasionally available from specialist breeders.

fish.breedingNotes: Males carry the eggs in a ventral brood pouch and give birth to live young after development inside the egg — a reproductive mode known as ovoviviparity. Captive breeding is occasionally achieved by specialist keepers, and captive-bred specimens are available from some dedicated breeders, though they remain uncommon.
